cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is asking what the history experts' statement proves about the "Happy Birthday" song. The options are A, B, C, D. First, I need to find the part of the text where history experts are mentioned. Let me skim through the passage again. The text says: "A number of history experts say that there is no record of who actually wrote the 'Happy Birthday to You' lyrics. Historians also say there is no way to know when the public began singing it, but they believe it was being sung long before it was printed and owned by a company." So, the key points here are that there's no record of the original author of the lyrics, and that the song was already in public use long before a company (like Warner Music Group) claimed ownership. The question is about what this proves. The options are: A) Does not belong to Warner Music Group B) Has more than 200 years' history C) Was initially owned by another company D) Has always been very popular Looking at the text, the song was written in 1893 by the Hill sisters as "Good Morning to All," and then the lyrics changed over time. The experts say there's no record of who wrote the "Happy Birthday" lyrics, and that the public was singing it before it was printed and owned by a company. Option B says it has more than 200 years of history. The song was written in 1893, so that's about 120 years ago (as of 2013, when the article might be from). So 200 years is too much. So B is probably not correct. Option C: Was initially owned by another company. The text doesn't mention another company initially owning it. Warner claims since 1988, but the experts say it was in the public domain before being owned by a company. So maybe there was no initial owner, hence C might not be correct. Option D: Has always been very popular. The text says it was being sung by the public long before being printed, but "has always been very popular" is a bit vague. The experts' statement isn't about popularity but about authorship and ownership. Option A: Does not belong to Warner Music Group. The lawyers argue that the history shows it belongs to the public. The experts' statement supports that because there's no record of ownership and it was in public use before Warner claimed it. So if there's no record of who wrote the lyrics and it was in public use, then the current copyright claim by Warner might be invalid. Therefore, the experts' statement would support that Warner doesn't have the right, hence A is correct. Wait, but the text says Warner has claimed the copyright
